content: Just spent 3 hours debugging a CSS grid issue that turned out to be a missing semicolon. I love this profession.
Hot take: most microservices architectures would be better as a well-structured monolith. Fight me.
The gap between 'AI can do X in a demo' and 'AI can reliably do X in production' is still massive. We need better validation, not better demos.
Writing docs tip: if you can't explain it in one sentence, you don't understand it well enough to document it yet.
New design system just dropped at work. 147 color tokens. One hundred and forty seven. We have 3 products.
Kubernetes tip: if your cluster needs a full-time engineer to keep running, you might not need Kubernetes.
Shipped a landing page this morning. 0 users, 0 revenue, infinite potential. This is the best part.
PSA: if your .env file is in your git history, rotating the keys isn't optional. Yes, even in a private repo.
@carol 100%. We rewrote our 'microservices' back into a monolith last quarter. Deployment went from 45 min to 3 min.
@alice exactly. The overhead of service boundaries only pays off when you actually have independent scaling needs.
Interesting paper on specification-driven development. The idea that you define 'why' before 'how' isn't new, but the tooling is finally catching up.
@elena do you have a link? I've been thinking about this exact problem — how do you validate AI output without manually reviewing everything?
Day 47 of my indie journey. Revenue: $0. Users: 12 (hi mom). Lessons learned: mass.
The best UI component is the one you delete. Seriously — every removal makes the remaining ones more important.

Incident postmortem template:
1. What happened
2. What we thought happened (wrong)
3. What actually happened
4. What we'll pretend we learned
Reviewed a PR today that had 'TODO: add authentication' in production code. The PR was 8 months old.
Started learning Svelte this week. Coming from React, the lack of boilerplate feels like I'm forgetting something.
@carol wait until you try SvelteKit — file-based routing will ruin you for everything else
Unpopular opinion: most 'AI-first' products are just CRUD apps with a chatbot stapled on. The hard part isn't the AI — it's knowing what to build.
Startup idea: an app that tracks how many startup ideas you've had vs how many you've actually built. I'll call it 'Shame'.
Just realized I've written more words in Slack this week than in actual documentation. This is a cry for help.
Dark mode isn't a feature. It's a baseline expectation. If your app doesn't have it in 2026, you're making a choice.
The scariest words in engineering: 'It works on my machine, let me just push to prod real quick'
Security tip of the day: your password manager's master password should not be 'password'. Yes, I've seen this. Yes, it was a security engineer.
Read a great thread about specification-driven validation. The key insight: when generation is cheap, validation is the bottleneck. Specs solve this.
@elena this is exactly the thesis behind RootSpec. Philosophy guides implementation, not the other way around.
Build in public update: added a landing page, a pricing page, and a 'coming soon' page. The product? Still in my head.
TypeScript trick: if you're writing 'as any' more than once per file, you're not using TypeScript. You're using JavaScript with extra steps.
Accessibility isn't a feature you add later. It's like structural integrity — if you skip it at the start, retrofitting costs 10x.
The best technical writing sounds like a smart friend explaining something at a whiteboard. Not a textbook. Not a tweet. A conversation.
